sourceFiles.name: impossible-rocket

fix.title: Correct propellant storage temperature to a physically valid Kelvin value
fix.description: This fix removes the thermodynamically impossible negative-Kelvin storage temperature by updating the FuelTank propellantTemperature attribute and the corresponding requirement text to a physically meaningful cryogenic value in Kelvin. Using 90.0 K is plausible for LOX-class cryogenic storage and resolves the contextual plausibility defect while keeping the model internally aligned between design data and requirement REQ_005.

changes.description: Updated the FuelTank propellantTemperature attribute from an impossible negative Kelvin value to a physically valid cryogenic storage temperature in Kelvin.
changes.after: attribute propellantTemperature : Temperature = 90.0;   // K — physically valid cryogenic storage temperature

changes.description: Revised REQ_005 so the requirement text matches the corrected physically plausible storage temperature and unit system.

results.name: 06_contextual_plausibility
results.rawText:
Model characterisation:
- **System purpose**: A launch vehicle/rocket system intended to deliver payload to a 550 km circular orbit and support guidance/landing-related performance claims.
- **Operational domain**: Aerospace / space launch.
- **Model scope**: A simplified system model / concept fragment describing major subsystems, selected performance attributes, a launch sequence, and a small set of requirements.
- **Known scope limitations**: The model is highly abstracted and omits detailed power, thermal, structural, communication, and control-loop architecture. Checks requiring detailed subsystem budgets or explicit interface definitions are assessed only where the model provides enough evidence; otherwise they are skipped rather than over-asserted.

ISSUE [PLAUSIBILITY-001]
Element:        ImpossibleRocket::FuelTank::propellantTemperature; ImpossibleRocket::REQ_005
Check:          1. Physical Plausibility
Finding:        The model specifies propellant storage at -300 K, which is physically impossible because absolute temperature cannot be below 0 K.
Evidence:       `attribute propellantTemperature : Temperature = -300.0; // K` and `REQ_005: "The propellant shall be stored at a temperature of -300 K."`
Domain Basis:   Thermodynamics forbids temperatures below absolute zero. No material or propellant can be stored at negative Kelvin.
Score:          0.0  -  Critical defect; the specified state is physically impossible.
Confidence:     HIGH
Impact:         Any thermal, storage, materials, or operational analysis using this value is invalid and could drive impossible design decisions.
Recommendation: Revise the temperature to a physically meaningful value and correct the requirement text; if a sub-zero Celsius value was intended, restate it in the correct unit system.

ISSUE [PLAUSIBILITY-002]
Element:        ImpossibleRocket::FuelTank
Check:          1. Physical Plausibility
Finding:        The specified burn duration is inconsistent with the stated propellant mass and mass flow rate. At 123000 kg and 273 kg/s, the burn would last about 451 s, not 162 s.
Evidence:       `attribute propellantMass : Mass = 123000.0;`, `attribute massFlowRate : Mass = 273.0;`, `attribute burnDuration : Duration = 162.0; // propellantMass / massFlowRate ≈ 450 s (stage 1)`
Domain Basis:   Propellant depletion time is mass divided by mass flow rate. 123000 / 273 ≈ 450.5 s, so the stated 162 s does not match the stated quantities.
Score:          0.2  -  Significant defect; core propulsion performance values are mutually inconsistent and likely to mislead mission analysis.
Confidence:     HIGH
Impact:         Trajectory, staging, acceleration, and mission-duration conclusions derived from these values will be unreliable.
Recommendation: Recalculate and align propellant mass, mass flow rate, and burn duration so all three describe the same propulsion regime.

ISSUE [PLAUSIBILITY-003]
Element:        ImpossibleRocket::ImpossibleRocketSystem; ImpossibleRocket::RocketStructure; ImpossibleRocket::FuelTank; ImpossibleRocket::PayloadFairing; ImpossibleRocket::MainEngine
Check:          1. Physical Plausibility
Finding:        The stated delta-v calculation appears to ignore major mass elements and is not plausible from the provided vehicle masses. Using the model's own masses, the final mass cited in the comment (`mf ≈ 30 300 kg`) is not consistent with dry structure, payload, fairing, tank empty mass, and engine mass already specified.
Evidence:       `attribute deltaV : Velocity = 8640.0; // m/s` with comment `m0 = 549 000 kg, mf ≈ 30 300 kg, ve = 3050 m/s → Δv ≈ 8 640 m/s`; compare with `stage1DryMass = 25600.0`, `stage2DryMass = 4500.0`, `payloadMass = 8500.0`, `fairingMass = 1850.0`, `emptyMass = 3200.0`, `engineMass = 470.0`.
Domain Basis:   Tsiolkovsky rocket equation requires a credible initial and final mass. The cited final mass is lower than the sum of several stated non-propellant masses, indicating the performance claim is not supported by the modelled mass properties.
Score:          0.1  -  Critical defect; the headline orbital performance claim is based on a non-credible mass basis.
Confidence:     HIGH
Impact:         Payload-to-orbit feasibility and architecture selection decisions could be materially wrong.
Recommendation: Provide a consistent mass breakdown by stage and event, then recompute delta-v from those masses; include which masses are jettisoned and when.

ISSUE [PLAUSIBILITY-004]
Element:        ImpossibleRocket::ImpossibleRocketSystem::overallEfficiency
Check:          1. Physical Plausibility
Finding:        The system-level overall efficiency of 0.91 is implausibly high for a launch vehicle if intended as overall mission or propulsion-chain efficiency, especially when the engine combustion efficiency alone is 0.94 and other losses must also exist.
Evidence:       `attribute combustionEfficiency : Efficiency = 0.94;` and `attribute overallEfficiency : Efficiency = 0.91; // 91% — realistic for high-Isp kerolox`
Domain Basis:   Compound efficiency across a full propulsion or vehicle system must be less than or equal to the product of contributing efficiencies and typically includes additional losses in pumps, expansion, mixture ratio mismatch, guidance, aero/gravity losses, etc. A 91% "overall" figure is not credible without a narrowly defined basis.
Score:          0.5  -  Moderate defect; the value may be a terminology error, but as written it supports an unrealistic system-performance interpretation.
Confidence:     MEDIUM  -  The concern depends on what "overallEfficiency" is intended to mean; the model does not define the efficiency boundary.
Impact:         Reviewers may overestimate vehicle performance or incorrectly compare this concept against real launch systems.
Recommendation: Define the efficiency boundary explicitly (e.g., combustion, propulsion, mission, or subsystem) and replace the value with one justified by an analysis.

ISSUE [PLAUSIBILITY-005]
Element:        ImpossibleRocket::PayloadFairing::separationAltitude; ImpossibleRocket::LaunchSequence
Check:          2. Timing & Sequencing Plausibility
Finding:        The fairing separation condition is typed and named inconsistently with the physical quantity involved: `separationAltitude` is declared as `Velocity` with a value of 110000.0. This creates a physically implausible interpretation if consumed literally as speed rather than altitude.
Evidence:       `attribute separationAltitude : Velocity = 110000.0; // m — above effective atmosphere (~110 km)` and action `fairingSeparation`.
Domain Basis:   Altitude and velocity are different physical dimensions. Sequencing logic based on the wrong dimension can invert or corrupt event timing conditions.
Score:          0.3  -  Significant defect; event logic tied to the wrong physical dimension can produce invalid sequence reasoning.
Confidence:     HIGH
Impact:         Analyses or downstream automation may trigger fairing separation using an impossible or wrong condition, affecting ascent safety and performance.
Recommendation: Retype the attribute to an altitude/length quantity and define the separation condition explicitly in the behaviour or requirements.

ISSUE [PLAUSIBILITY-006]
Element:        ImpossibleRocket::ImpossibleRocketSystem::maxAltitude
Check:          1. Physical Plausibility
Finding:        The attribute `maxAltitude` is declared as `Velocity` even though its meaning and units in the comment are altitude in metres. This is a dimensional mismatch that makes the stated orbital condition physically ambiguous.
Evidence:       `attribute maxAltitude : Velocity = 550000.0; // m — 550 km LEO insertion orbit`
Domain Basis:   Physical plausibility depends on correct dimensional modelling. Altitude is a spatial quantity, not a velocity.
Score:          0.4  -  Significant defect; the orbital-state claim is represented with the wrong physical dimension, undermining analysis credibility.
Confidence:     HIGH
Impact:         Orbit insertion, mission analysis, and requirement traceability can be misinterpreted or processed incorrectly.
Recommendation: Introduce a proper altitude/length value type and retag this attribute accordingly.

ISSUE [PLAUSIBILITY-007]
Element:        ImpossibleRocket::GuidanceSystem::navigationAccuracy
Check:          1. Physical Plausibility
Finding:        The guidance accuracy is represented as `Velocity = 10.0` while the comment states `10 m` landing accuracy. This is a dimensional error that makes the guidance claim physically incoherent as modelled.
Evidence:       `attribute navigationAccuracy : Velocity = 10.0; // m — 3-sigma landing accuracy, SpaceX-class`
Domain Basis:   Position accuracy is a length/error metric, not a velocity. Using the wrong dimension compromises physical interpretation and any calculations using the value.
Score:          0.4  -  Significant defect; the requirement may be sensible, but the model encodes it as the wrong physical quantity.
Confidence:     HIGH
Impact:         Verification and control-performance analyses may use the wrong units and reach incorrect conclusions about landing capability.
Recommendation: Replace the type with a position/length accuracy value type and align the requirement satisfaction accordingly.

CHECK PASSED: 3. Environmental Plausibility  -  Aside from the explicit impossible temperature already recorded under physical plausibility, the model does not provide enough detailed environmental exposure, materials, EMC, vibration, humidity, or radiation assumptions to justify additional environmental findings.

CHECK PASSED: 4. Operational Plausibility  -  The operational concept is minimal but not inherently implausible; no human workload, maintenance, uptime, or logistics claims are modelled at a level that supports a specific defect.

CHECK PASSED: 5. External System & Environment Assumptions  -  The model does not materially depend on external services, communications, or licensed spectrum assumptions, so no explicit external-assumption plausibility issue is evidenced.

CHECK PASSED: 6. Architectural Plausibility  -  The named subsystems and stated headline technologies are broadly consistent with existing launch-vehicle practice, and the model does not specify integration details that would justify a stronger architectural infeasibility claim beyond the recorded quantity inconsistencies.

CHECK PASSED: 7. Causal Plausibility  -  The launch sequence is ordered in a causally sensible direction and no explicit circular constraints or faster-than-physical propagation claims are modelled.

| ID | Score | Confidence | One-line description |
|---|---:|---|---|
| PLAUSIBILITY-001 | 0.0 | HIGH | Propellant temperature and requirement specify impossible negative Kelvin storage. |
| PLAUSIBILITY-002 | 0.2 | HIGH | Burn duration does not match stated propellant mass and mass flow rate. |
| PLAUSIBILITY-003 | 0.1 | HIGH | Delta-v claim relies on a final mass inconsistent with the modelled vehicle masses. |
| PLAUSIBILITY-004 | 0.5 | MEDIUM | System overall efficiency is implausibly high or insufficiently defined. |
| PLAUSIBILITY-005 | 0.3 | HIGH | Fairing separation altitude is modelled as a velocity quantity. |
| PLAUSIBILITY-006 | 0.4 | HIGH | Max altitude is typed as velocity, making orbital-state modelling physically ambiguous. |
| PLAUSIBILITY-007 | 0.4 | HIGH | Navigation accuracy is modelled as velocity instead of position accuracy. |

Overall Assessment Score: 0.27  
Model Status: Critical deficiencies — major rework required before this model can be used.
